Lesson: Lesson 1 - Ground - Introduction to Pilot Training
Instructor Remarks: Student works at Pratt Whitney full time. Wants to do this 2-3x/month, and gradually move up to every week. He wants to become an airline pilot. Has been studying at home and has a good grasp on ground knowledge.

Overall a great flight! XC flight planning was incomplete but was well done with what was complete. Winds were slightly different than forecasted which was good for the student to see in comparison to their calculations. Student did a nice job of thinking ahead, but needs more practice with the efficiency of staying ahead of the airplane in terms of checklists and landing briefings. The student was a bit delayed with these items even though he was always thinking of what comes next. 

Student got good practice with pilotage, dead reckoning, auto pilot (KAP 140) use, and tuning into the ENE VOR. We also discussed how it's important to think about where you would want to go in the case of an emergency while enroute if an emergency was to arise.

Overall a great pattern work flight practicing normal TOLs and the power off 180. Student consistently is too high on final and that is directly related to the student neglecting to reduce power when abeam the touchdown point to start an initial descent. This was discussed in the debrief to make the pattern "symmetrical" in the sense that you want to turn base about 300' below TPA as you turn crosswind about 300' below TPA. Student is also better understanding how to use the aim point in order to consistently hit their touchdown point. Student was a bit slow on a few approaches due to pitching the nose up without adding power, but this point was reviewed. The biggest issue with today's landings were the incorrect use of crosswind correction!! We had a direct crosswind and the ailerons were turned in the wrong direction after every landings. 

Power off 180s were introduced today on the ground and the execution was rather good for the first few attempts. Consistently hit designated touchdown point!

Lesson: Lesson 12 - Flight - Instrument Flying and Local Navigation
Instructor Remarks:
Best glide is important as it is the speed with the lowest total drag. Think of the L/D Max graph.

Gyroscopic Precession is felt 90 degrees ahead in the direction of rotation. When you think of the prop spinning clockwise, if we could reach out and pull on the top of the prop to rotate the nose up, that force would be felt a quarter of a rotation in the same direction as the prop is spinning. So even though we "pulled" on the top of the propeller, the plane will feel the pull on the right.

XC to KAUG using pilotage primarily. Student practiced inputting flight into GPS and using auto pilot. Student also practiced proper XC checklist usage and briefings (takeoff and landing for example). Overall, student did a great job using pilotage and his map to navigate to the destination, but still needs assistance with the nav log for proper calculations in order to use dead reckoning properly for navigation. This will be addressed in an upcoming lesson. 

GPS usage, auto pilot introduction and briefings were all solid. Student tends to over control the airplane in flight especially while multi tasking. We discussed how we should be flying with minimal pressure and to be aware that he tends to inadvertently maneuvers the airplane when he looks anywhere but outside. Student still needs to work on overall situational awareness in terms of staying ahead of the airplane, and positioning the aircraft as to not be a potential conflict with other traffic (entering and exiting the pattern mostly). Overall, student is getting better at managing himself during high workload phases of flight and his briefings/checklist usage are becoming more fluent (get the weather, choose the runway, brief the descent/how to enter the pattern/winds/runway length, final approach speed, flap setting). Student just needs more practice and more confidence in these departments.

Student landings were safe, but need more cross wind correction to avoid drift and/or side loading!

Instructor Remarks:
LESSON 47 of NEW SMA SYLLABUS (checkride prep): 

General Notes:
The other general notes is that the student should be briefing his landings more than he is (type of landing, runway being used, how to enter pattern wind velocity and direction, flap setting to be used, final approach speed) and student would benefit from announcing checklist to be used out loud and checklist completion ("climb checklist" ..... "climb checklist complete"). Student's airmanship and decision making are very good! He is a confident pilot always looking for feedback. All notes are minor and are able to easily be fixed with some practice.

Emergency Procedures - Emergency Approach and Landing:
Student hesitated to find a proper field. His selection process was good by assessing every option properly, but was not timely. Selected field was small and would've been extremely difficult to land in. Student also was a bit fast when transitioning from emergency descent to best glide thus destroying endurance. We debriefed how to select a field, immediately get over landing spot and maneuver to continuously keep yourself in a position to land. We also discussed the proper use of emergency checklists and to use them if and only if there is ample time. If not, keep the important memory items will be essential! 

Emergency Descent:
Gave student an engine fire on the left side of the engine. Student turned into the first instead of away. However, student maintained 100 knots very well and ran through memory items for the fire accordingly. 

Power off Stall:
Stall entrance and recovery were very well performed. Student should announce impending stall and needed more directional control (heading control) during recovery. 

Short field Landing:
Student needs a bit more speed control on short final. Got slow on the second attempt and came short of touchdown point. Overall, sight picture is consistent, and airmanship is solid!

Short field Takeoff:
Student needs to solidify his rotation and climbout speeds. Execution was exceptional w/ some instruction.

Instructor Remarks:
Landing Notes:
Remember aim point and touchdown point (these should be briefed!). Use your briefed aim point to manage your energy accordingly. One approach was low and flat, the other high and steep. Airspeed is ALWAYS kings when it comes to setting up for a successful landing! If the landing isn't stable, you're not +10/-5 knots, or you just don't like the setup.... GO-AROUND!

If you're set up correctly on your aim point, keep power setting in until about a half-centerline stripe before reaching your aim point and then begin your round-out and flare! Power can be used carefully to extend your round-out/flare phase if necessary.

Lesson 26 - STAGE CHECK II: PRE-SOLO CHECK
Instructor Remarks: Today we only did the take offs and landings portion of the stage check. Stayed in the pattern and did 7 take offs/landings and one go around. Runway and taxiway surfaces were covered with 1/8in snow, breaking action 5/5/5. Winds were calm at the beginning of the session and increased to 5-7 knots at the end. Take offs were good, with good directional control and good rotation and climb out. Traffic awareness and radio communications was good. First two landings were a little flat, but got better as the session went on. Last two landings were the best. Go around was good with prompt application of full power, good management of aircraft pitch/yaw and flaps retraction. Note: Make sure to keep right hand on the throttle for the duration of the landing (in case of a go around and to make sure power comes back to full idle once rw is made).

Instructor Remarks:
During taxi from the ramp to runway seven no CTAF call was made crossing 1432 at bravo nor did he look at the approach end of 14 or the approach end of 32 and the runway.  
Taxi speed on Charlie was 17 kts with patchy Ice and packed snow on 20% of the taxi away ￼. Turning on foxtrot to position the airplane into the wind for run up checks was too fast. I assisted stopping the turn as it was heading for deep snow and taxiway light￼.
There was no awareness of wind conditions during the takeoff. Flight ￼controls weren’t in the proper position. 
During a demonstration of a power on stall, Michael said he was having difficulty stalling the airplane. There was 3 inches of travel remaining in the control wheel.  When the airplane did develop a full stall, the left wing dipped significantly as Michael tried to pick it up by fully deflecting the a￼ilerons. No rudder was used for directional control. 
During a power off stall, a stabilized descent was not established, the recovery did not include stabilizing the airplane at VX or VY for a go around. 
300 foot deviations of altitude were noted during slow flight.
Michael needed to be reminded to raise the wing and look before almost every turn he started.￼
We entered the traffic pattern for seven midfield at 2000 feet indicated altitude   ￼

Lesson: Lesson 39 - STAGE CHECK III - Solo Cross Country Check
Instructor Remarks: Completed the Stage check successful. Started with a take off on x-c to KHVN up to cruise altitude, open VFR flight plan (simulated), weather scenario, diversion to KDAW, normal landing to a full stop. Normal take off, headed to well beach area, en route simulated electrical fire, steep turns, slow flight power off stalls x2, power on stalls, then emergency engine out. Headed back to KSFM, pattern work, go around, simulated engine out to a full stop landing RW 07.

Bumpy and gusty day. We reviewed some of the ground areas and the x-c planning to Hartford. Took off on the flight plan, climbed en route, requested FF, leveled off 4500ft and leaned engine. Simulated weather scenario, decided to divert to KDAW. One go around, one good full stop landing there. Took off, to the wells beach area, simulated engine and electric fire on the way there. Steep turns, slow flight, power on and off stalls (straight away and turning). Simulated engine out, then S turns and turn around a point. Back to KSFM for take offs and landings.

Lesson: Lesson 10 - Flight - Flight Skills Review and Radio Instruction

Instructor Remarks: Non-SMA student. This was a ground only session (3.0) to assess currency and to prepare for a flight review and instrument proficiency check.

This was non-SMA training conducted in the customer’s airplane (PA-28 N2189Z), KPSM-KPSM, 2.5 hours. Greg Weltin is an experienced GA pilot and current for VFR operations but not IFR current. Although Greg’s basic aircraft handling and general procedures were standard, the result of this lesson was dual instruction for currency without an IPC due to Greg’s lack of standardized  procedures.

We conducted a thorough ground session the previous day to assess Greg’s currency and knowledge base. The ground training was also conducted in accordance with the requirements of a flight review and an Instrument Proficiency Check (IPC). 

Greg’s general knowledge was pretty good, though a little weak when discussing regulatory requirements. Greg was well prepared and ready to train. It was a good ground session.

We conducted the flight portion the next day (09/20/2025) out of Port City Air at KPSM when Greg’s airplane cleared maintenance for its bi-annual instrument checks. We briefed and filed for the planned instrument round robin from the Plan of Action that I had provided prior to our training.

Greg is very familiar with his airplane and his preflight aircraft preparation was well above average. Throughout the flight Greg’s basic aircraft handling was also above average and his basic attitude instrument flying was also quite good; however, procedurally Greg was well below average to UNSAT. It was clear that Greg is non-instrument current because he almost never flies IFR or practices instrument procedures. In fact, Greg had not flown an instrument procedure since his last IPC ten months ago. 

The result of this flight was dual received as the instrument procedures were UNSAT at best and definitely unsafe.  

Greg is considering whether or not he intends to pursue instrument currency and proficiency. He elected to fly commercial for the trip he had planned to take this week. I told Greg that I would concoct a training plan that would involve at least two RedBIRD session and at least three flights followed by an IPC conducted by a different instructor.

Lesson 13 - STAGE CHECK I: FLIGHT FUNDAMENTALS
Instructor Remarks: We were unable to fly due to weather. Completed as much ground as possible.

This was our first proficiency flight with emphasis on basic attitude instrument flying, instrument navigation without approaches, and checklist usage.

Greg’s basic attitude instrument flying is very good. We experimented with and noted pitch and power setting that may serve useful for controlled climbs and descents. 

Greg has dedicated a lot of time and effort into developing useable checklists appropriate to phase of flight that are POH compliant. Greg has made excellent progress with checklist usage in the airplane by keeping the checklists handy and visible and focusing on appropriate “triggers” for each phase of flight. Great improvement!

Basic navigation skills were also greatly improved with appropriate use of the CDI for navigation, NAVAIDs tuned ad identified, and course tracking above average. The move from the RedBird to Greg’s own airplane really helped because it eliminated the artificiality of using a “similar” cockpit set-up. It was so much more useful for Greg to manipulate the avionics of his own airplane, not just because he is more familiar but because these are the skills and habits that need to be reinforced. 

WINGS CREDIT for activity A100126-54 for ASEL Instrument Maneuvers was given.

Instructor Remarks:
This was Greg’s second proficiency flight of our three-flight syllabus. This was a round robin filed and flown IFR within the ATC system (LPV RWY32 KASH, VOR-A KCON, ILS RWY07 KSFM). The objective was to consolidate previous RedBird and flight training incorporating ATC procedures and Instrument Approach Procedures (IAP).

Greg’s above average basic attitude instrument flying served him well and all phases of flight were well within the ACS standards. We reviewed ACS 91-73B, Single Pilot Taxi Operations, and discussed the importance of prioritizing tasks, such a when and where to copy an IFR clearance or configure the airplane. 

Greg’s ATC communications were adequate but weak and reflect his lack of currency. This will improve as Greg gets more exposure the the ATC system. 

Greg’s checklist usage has become very good. He now has available and uses POH compliant checklists, although he needs more practice (repetitions) to get comfortable using them. We discussed the benefit of annunciating each checklist item to slow down his pace and to ensure that each item is actually accomplished. His checklist discipline is a work in progress but steadily improving. 

This was a very good, if busy, flight and Greg did very well. We will move on to the third and final flight in our syllabus to consolidate the course objective to return Greg to instrument proficiency. 


WINGS credit for activity A070405-166 ASEL-Instrument Procedures  was given.
2.0 GND/ 2.3 dual

Lesson 47 - Flight - Final ACS Maneuvers Review
Lesson 47 - Flight - Final ACS Maneuvers Review
Instructor Remarks: Short and soft field operations, go-around, power on and off stalls, steep turns

This was the commercial instrument review that we briefed 01/05/2026 but were unable to fly due to aircraft maintenance.

Conducted a climb demo at 90KIAS and at 60KIAS to demonstrate the change in rudder input required with a steep deck angle and slow airspeed such as at the top of a chandelle.

Conducted numerous Lazy eights with an emphasis on slowing down everything. With repetition Jed relaxed and got into a pretty good rhythm with the mechanics, although right rudder control was frequently lacking.

Demonstrated an accelerated stall from 60KIAS level with 60degrees AOB simply to show the increased stall speed level with AOB. Demonstrated and approach turn stall with emphasis on the importance of not over banking (60 AOB?) in uncoordinated flight for spin awareness and prevention.
Discussed the importance of considering the increased stall speed while using high angles of bank during steep spirals. Debriefed the SMA SOP suggested airspeeds.

Worked through four accelerated stalls per the ACS entered at 80KIAS, 45AOB and pull to the stall. We emphasized the importance of reducing the AOA immediately while deliberately rolling to wings level and adding power. 

Induced a simulated fire in the backseat that continued to burn, necessitating an immediate landing. Jed used the checklists and pretty methodically worked through an unusual, surprise emergency, including getting out a MAYDAY call.   

Jed accomplished an excellent power off 180 and put the airplane right on his target. I debriefed him on the proper entry and airspeed and cautioned Jed to stick to the Airplane Flying Handbook and not deploy flaps at the abeam but wait until the 90 or when the landing is assured. This maneuver should begin as your best ever, power off glide! 

Jed is a very conscientious learner and is always well prepared; however, I cautioned him about a few bad habits that have crept into his flying;

Proper brake check per the Airplane Flying Manual when leaving the chocks
Taxi chart visible and taxi route briefed both outbound and on taxi-in
Continuous use of checklists appropriate for the phase of flight
Not configuring the aircraft after landing until clear of the runway
Familiarization with and adherence to SMA SOP (OPS SPECS)

Jed’s aircraft handling is quite good. To complete his training Jed would benefit from a procedural review with the Airplane Flying Handbook and the Commercial Pilot ACS to ensure that he is within the ACS standards.
